lesson: The # (h1) heading is Slide 1. The intro paragraph immediately below it is the slide body. <!-- Slide 1 --> goes after that paragraph, before the first --- separator. Miscounting here leads to slide total mismatches.
A short recap slide (≤2 min) bridging from the previous lecture's limitations works better than jumping straight into new syntax. It gives students a motivation frame before content starts.

The instructor's PDF uses questions as slide titles (e.g. 'What's the big idea?', 'That sounds like an if statement…'). Apply this question-as-hook pattern more consistently on introductory slides — it mirrors the source material's style and pulls students in.

When a lecture introduces multiple variants of the same concept (e.g. three loop types), end with a 'which one to use?' decision framework before the lab. Students need synthesis before application — skipping it produces guessing, not reasoning.

The intro paragraph under a topic's # heading should appear on a separate ## slide with the same title, not on the # slide itself. In Reveal.js the # slide is a vertical navigation parent — putting content there makes it easy to skip.

When a slide needs both a concept explanation and a code example, use two consecutive ## slides with the same title: first slide has the concept as bullets, second slide has the code. This is the implementation of the 'no text on both sides of code' rule.

Use ::: {.columns} / ::: {.column width="50%"} / ::: / ::: for two-column layout. Code blocks inside columns require ```{.cpp} (dot notation) not ```cpp. Close each column with ::: and the outer container with ::: (trailing space). Use for side-by-side code comparison and multi-choice scenario slides.

When you have a code example do not have text on the same page unless the example is very short (<6 lines). Even then, have text either above or below the code example but not both. It’s OK to add another slide if needed.
Prefer lists to blocks of text.

Opening slides should contain no prose paragraphs. The opening slide poses the question and stops — one short question headline plus at most one or two bullets. Any explanatory paragraph belongs on a subsequent slide.

Opening question titles should be headline-length fragments, not full interrogative sentences. 'Moving a loop through every element in a collection?' not 'How does a loop move through every element in a collection?'
Summary slides should use bullet lists, not prose paragraphs. Each bullet captures one takeaway. Drop trailing wrap-up sentences.
Remove trailing explanatory sentences after bullet lists. If the bullets are clear, the sentence restating their logic is filler.
Add dot-padded inline comments to code examples to narrate specific lines (e.g. //.......... outer loop). This turns code into annotated reading material instead of a block students have to decode.

For visual or structural concepts, show the concrete artifact first and let students process it before naming the concept. A bare 3x3 table posing the problem, then the answer on the next slide, is more effective than a text question.
Use nested sub-bullets for hierarchical content. Do not pack sub-items inline after an em-dash on the same bullet line.
Use # (h1) for Last Time and Today's Agenda slides so they become proper horizontal sections alongside topic columns, not vertical slides in an implicit section 0.
On code-heavy slides, explanatory bullets that annotate the code belong in speaker notes, not below the code block. Post-code bullets create text-on-both-sides violations and clutter the visual.

For topics that introduce a concept students already know in another form (e.g. arrays when students know vectors), open with an explicit contrast framing — 'same idea, different contract' — rather than a neutral definition. It orients students faster and reduces the feeling that they're starting from zero.

When a topic has a direct 'which one to use?' decision (e.g. index-based for loop vs. range-for), put the decision framework on its own slide near the end of the topic — after students have seen both options — rather than burying it in bullets on an earlier slide. Students need to see the options before they can reason about the choice.

When introducing an algorithm that modifies data in place (e.g. swap in bubble sort), show the broken version first — what happens if you skip the temp variable — before showing the correct version. The failure mode is more memorable than a rule stated up front.

When a topic has a pointer/value gotcha that will bite students immediately (e.g. array decay on function arguments), plant a one-slide preview of the problem in the preceding topic rather than waiting for the full explanation. Students encounter the surprise in a low-stakes context first, so the detailed explanation lands on already-primed minds.

Slide titles are labels, not sentence fragments. Do not append descriptors after an em-dash (e.g. 'Reading the Nth record — the formula' → 'Reading the Nth record'). The title names the slide; the content explains it.

When the intro slide contains both a motivating hook and a taxonomy or categorization of the topic (e.g. 'two kinds of…'), split them into separate slides. The intro slide keeps the hook only; the taxonomy gets its own slide with a descriptive title.
Even two short motivating sentences on an intro slide should be formatted as bullet points (+), not run together as prose. Each statement gets its own line.

When starting a rebuild session, read the most recently completed lecture's topic files before writing any slides — title format, bullet style, notes placement, and summary structure all drift over time and the latest lecture is the authoritative style reference.

For the first lecture in a sequence, replace # Last Time in the stitched file with a welcome or context slide — there is no prior lecture to recap, and a blank incremental list creates a confusing empty slide.

When highlighting statements inside a code block that contains blank lines, use pipe-separated ranges in code-line-numbers (e.g., "10-11|13-14") rather than a continuous range — a continuous range that spans blank lines highlights the blanks and weakens the visual focus.

When old lecture materials contain a hand-drawn or image-based diagram, reproduce it as a Markdown table or labeled text layout in the slide body, and note a graphical redraw suggestion in speaker notes — this keeps the content editable and version-controlled without blocking on image production.

When an instructor uploads old slide materials mid-session to augment a topic, present a revised slide list for that topic only before generating — do not restart the full proposal cycle for unchanged topics.

When two # (h1) headings appear consecutively in a stitched file, do not place a --- separator between them — Quarto/Reveal.js will render the separator as a blank slide. Let the # heading itself act as the section boundary.

Setting navigation-mode: linear in _quarto.yml produces a traditional left-to-right linear presentation. The # (h1) headings still render as visual section-title slides but no longer create horizontal columns. The custom keyboard JS written for vertical mode's section-boundary problem can be removed entirely — standard Reveal.js keyboard behavior handles everything.

When _quarto.yml has project: type: default, Quarto validates all {{< include >}} targets across the entire project before rendering any single file. Missing include targets in unrebuilt lectures block rendering of everything. Fix: create minimal stub .qmd files at each missing path — one HTML comment line is sufficient.

index.html link text should be the lecture title only — never include week number or lecture number. The card already displays the badge (e.g. 01.01) and slot (Lecture 1), so prefixing the title with 'Week 1, Lecture 01 –' is redundant.

When moving a topic earlier from a later lecture, adapt examples to the receiving lecture's prerequisite level instead of copying the old topic wholesale. For terminal colors in 02.02, keep ANSI constants but remove vector/range-for examples.

When a lecture is reframed around a design theme, keep the agenda limited to instructional topics and move out-of-sequence material such as scope or shadow variables to the later topic where it belongs.
When introducing vectors before index-based loops, teach index notation only as access to one known position, then use its limits to motivate range-based for loops.
When a slide combines bullet/text explanation with a table of five or more data rows, shrink the explanatory text or use a two-column layout so the table remains readable.
When a mixed explanation/code slide has ten or more total units, counting bullet sentences plus lines of code, split it into separate explanation and code slides.

Build lectures as a linear flow through the deck: when introducing a new programming construct, connect it to prior design models before syntax, showing what stays the same in the IPO model and what changes in the execution trace.
When a familiar concept becomes newly important in a later design context, frame it as consequences rather than reteaching it from zero.
Use {.smaller} only for opening/topic-hook slides unless a specific slide truly needs scaling; applying it broadly can make Reveal.js fit slides too tall and hide titles.
When a prior lecture introduces a model used by the next lecture, include a brief model-check slide early in the first topic rather than making the model a full topic again.
When a lecture topic is a synthesis of prior mechanics, frame it around design choices and consequences rather than reteaching the underlying syntax.
When a lecture introduces design patterns before the full OOP unit, keep the pattern focus on problem, structure, and tradeoff; use minimal C++ code shapes only after the design intent is clear.

When rebuilding from an older PDF that includes exercises, first separate the instructional spine from activity blocks; keep exercises out of the agenda and main topic files unless explicitly requested.
When students know range-based traversal but not index access, teach the index model as a separate topic before introducing index-controlled loops.
When familiar material receives new terminology, explicitly establish that it is the same concept before concentrating on genuinely new applications.
